blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following formula:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: This equation expresses the total differential of a function in terms of its partial derivatives.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$, $y$, and $z$.

blocks.latex: \text{total change in } z = \text{change in } z \text{ due to the change in } x + \text{change in } z \text{ due to the change in } y.
blocks.description: This equation summarizes the total change in a function based on variations in its variables.

blocks.text: Let $P$ be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let $P$ move to a new position $Q$ near to $P$.
The change of position is denoted by $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$, which can be related to the change in $z$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21
blocks.description: The figure illustrates the relationship between the changes in positions $P$ to $Q$ on the surface and how it corresponds to the change in $z$.
